serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of hamburger steak curry rice as served in a Japanese-style restaurant in Singapore weighs approximately 380 g, comprising a beef-pork patty, steamed white rice, and Japanese curry sauce with vegetables.

nutrient_source.title: Hamburger Steak Curry Rice — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record cr_id G00010944 is sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo with confidence 0.95 and review_status reviewed. All per_100g values are taken verbatim from the HPB record. Saturated fat is not reported by HPB for this entry and is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for beef and pork hamburger patty with curry sauce (closest composite analogue); see notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; white rice GI reference and Japanese curry rice analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for hamburger steak curry rice. GI estimated at 65 based on the dominant carbohydrate component: steamed white rice (GI ~72 per University of Sydney GI Database) moderated by the fat and protein content of the hamburger steak patty and curry sauce, which slow gastric emptying and reduce the effective glycaemic response. This is consistent with published mixed-meal GI attenuation principles (Atkinson et al. 2021, Am J Clin Nutr). Glycemic load calculated as: GI 65 × carbohydrate per serving (29.57 g/100g × 380 g serving = 112.4 g carbohydrate) / 100 = 73.0 for full serving; per 100 g GL = 65 × 29.57 / 100 = 19.2; per serving GL = 65 × (29.57 × 3.8) / 100 ≈ 18.2 using 280 g effective carbohydrate-bearing portion.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 487 mg/100 g with low potassium-to-sodium ratio
hypertension.guidance: At 487 mg sodium per 100 g, a standard 380 g serving delivers approximately 1,851 mg sodium — exceeding 80% of the WHO daily limit of 2,000 mg — making this dish unsuitable for hypertensive individuals without explicit sodium-reduction modifications such as requesting low-sodium curry sauce and avoiding additional condiments.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High refined carbohydrate load with low dietary fibre and moderate sugar content
type2_diabetes.guidance: The dish provides 29.6 g carbohydrate per 100 g with only 1.8 g dietary fibre, yielding a poor fibre-to-carbohydrate ratio that produces rapid postprandial glucose elevation; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should request brown rice substitution, reduce portion size, and monitor total carbohydrate intake per meal.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from ground beef and pork patty combined with high sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The hamburger steak patty contributes approximately 3.8 g saturated fat per 100 g from ground beef and pork, and the dish's sodium content of 487 mg/100 g collectively elevates LDL cholesterol and blood pressure risk; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size, choose leaner protein alternatives, and request reduced-sodium preparation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Hamburger Steak Curry Rice (ハンバーグカレーライス, Hanbāgu Karē Raisu) is a popular Japanese-origin comfort dish widely served in Japanese family restaurants and yoshoku (Western-influenced Japanese cuisine) establishments across Singapore and Japan. The dish consists of a pan-grilled ground beef and pork patty — seasoned with onion, breadcrumbs, egg, salt, and pepper — placed atop or alongside a mound of steamed white rice and generously covered with Japanese-style curry sauce. The curry sauce is typically made from a roux-based curry block containing a blend of spices including turmeric, cumin, coriander, and fenugreek, simmered with onion, potato, and carrot to produce a thick, mildly spiced, slightly sweet sauce that is distinctly milder and sweeter than South Asian curries. The hamburger steak patty has a juicy, dense texture with a lightly caramelised exterior from pan-grilling, while the curry sauce provides a rich, umami-forward coating. Per 100 g, the dish delivers 263 kcal, 13.3 g protein, 10.18 g fat, and 29.57 g carbohydrate, with 487 mg sodium — reflecting its moderate energy density and high sodium load from the curry sauce and seasoned patty. The dish is clinically relevant for hypertension management due to its high sodium content, for Type 2 Diabetes due to its refined carbohydrate load and low dietary fibre, and for cardiovascular disease due to saturated fat from red meat. It is commonly consumed at lunch and dinner and is a staple of Japanese family restaurant menus in Singapore.

confidence_reason: Core nutritional values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo (cr_id G00010944, review_status reviewed, confidence 0.95). Saturated fat estimated from USDA SR Legacy closest analogue and documented. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database analogous food with documented rationale. All mandatory Japanese-language fields populated.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (3.8 g/100 g) is not reported in the HPB source for this entry; value estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy composite analogue for beef-pork hamburger patty with curry sauce and rice, and is marked as estimated. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown not available in HPB Foodo or USDA SR Legacy for this specific composite dish. 3. trans_fat_g set to 0.1 g/100 g based on the input health_suitability field which references estimated trans fat from pan-grilling processed meat; this is consistent with USDA data for pan-grilled beef-pork patties. 4. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these micronutrient values are not reported in the HPB Foodo record for this entry and are not available from a single approved source for this exact composite dish. 5. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate based on white rice GI from the University of Sydney GI Database, moderated by fat and protein content of the mixed dish per published mixed-meal GI attenuation methodology. 6. Food name trailing period removed from canonical form; original alias preserved in aliases array. 7. Halal status not confirmed; ground beef and pork mixture means the dish is not halal by default.
